title: Quiz: Gate Merging and Rotation Combining
description: Test your understanding of gate merging techniques and commutation rules

questions.question: What is the result of merging RZ(0.3) followed by RZ(0.5) on the same qubit?

options.text: Two separate RZ gates that cannot be merged

questions.explanation: Same-axis rotations add their angles: RZ(0.3) * RZ(0.5) = RZ(0.3 + 0.5) = RZ(0.8). This is because rotation matrices about the same axis compose by adding angles.

questions.question: Can RX(theta) and RZ(phi) gates on the same qubit be directly merged into a single rotation gate?

options.text: No, rotations about different axes cannot be combined by adding angles

questions.explanation: Rotations about different axes do not simply add angles. RX and RZ can be merged into a single U3 gate using Euler decomposition, but not by adding angles. The composition of rotations about different axes requires matrix multiplication.

questions.question: Which gates commute with each other on the same qubit?

options.text: RZ, S, T, and Z (all diagonal gates)

questions.explanation: Diagonal gates (RZ, S, T, Z, P, U1) all commute with each other because they are all diagonal in the computational basis. Non-diagonal gates like H, X, RX do not generally commute with each other or with diagonal gates.

questions.question: The sequence T-T-T-T on a qubit can be simplified to:
questions.explanation: T gate adds phase pi/4. Four T gates add 4 * pi/4 = pi phase, which equals the Z gate. T^2 = S and T^4 = Z.

questions.question: RZ gates on the control qubit of a CNOT commute with that CNOT gate.
questions.explanation: Z-rotations on the control qubit commute with CNOT because they only add a phase based on the control qubit state, which does not affect the controlled-X operation. This is because Z is diagonal and CNOT applies X only when the control is |1>.

questions.question: What decomposition is used to merge arbitrary single-qubit gates into one gate?
questions.explanation: Euler decomposition (typically ZYZ or ZXZ) expresses any single-qubit unitary as U = e^(i*alpha) * Rz(phi) * Ry(theta) * Rz(lambda), allowing arbitrary single-qubit gate sequences to be merged into a single U3 gate.
